Once our credit was pulled & the car showed up on the credit, our credit consultant asked to see the contract for the car. Once receipt of the contract, I noticed there was information that was not true & information that I did not have knowledge of. The down payment amount was not correct. The contract showed a down payment of {$1000.00} when in fact we put {$1200.00} down. The rep we spoke with ( XXXX XXXX ) stated that he needed to pass the information along to someone above him and he would have someone from the finance or funding department to contact us. At this time we were placed on hold & were told the department we needed to speak with was on another call & would be with us shortly. After holding for 5 mins, XXXX came to the line asking if anyone had contacted us and once told no he stated he would go to the person that was to contact us & make them aware that we were waiting to hear from them. After speaking with XXXX, we then decided to email him. This email informed the company that they seemed unsure about how to handle such a issue therefore, we thought it may help if we told them what we wanted to happen due to this fraudulent contract. We let it be known that we wanted out of the contract with no negative remarks on our credit, we wanted all payments made returned and we wanted them to retrieve their car. 2 hours later we received a email from XXXX XXXX with her direct phone # and asking that I contact her at our convenience. I told her about the issue with not having a tag and not being able to drive the car for the first 5 months. She was informed that I tried to see if the dealer had reopened before contacting them. I confirmed there was no car traded in, I informed her the down payment amount on the contract was incorrect and she was also informed that the car that the dealer said was traded is not a car that we own. She asked if the dealer told us to lie to them during the welcome call about the down payment amount and we told her no. We explained that the dealer advised us that the down payment amount that would be stated in the welcome call was because of the way the dealer was going to split the down payment up among the GAP coverage and warranty. XXXX stated 3-4 different times during the call that the contract was definitely fraud. Her exact words each time was " oh, yes maam, this is definitely fraud on their end ''. The call ended by XXXX saying she needed to speak with her manager, let them know what we wanted and she promised to get back with us the nest day. As XXXX did contact me back the next day, she then changed from what she said the previous day and said there was no fraud. When asked why she was no changing what she said from the previous call, she denied ever saying that she admitted the contract was fraudulent and then asked why I kept bringing up yesterdays conversation. Once I realized she seemed to be doing just as dirty as the dealership, I then let her know that the company could keep the money that had been paid on the car but I wanted out of the contract because doing business with their company has been nothing but a headache. We ended the call by XXXX saying she would pass the information of me wanting out of the contract with no negative remarks on my credit even if there was no refund of payments to her manager and " if he would like '', he would contact me. 
XXXX was representing the finance company and stated herself the contract was fraudulent but then after she spoke with other people in the company, she denied ever saying that. Then stated that I was asked about having another car during a welcome call but no we were not.

Consumer Claim:
American express ( along with other credit card companies ) have a policy of updating merchants who post recurring transactions with new consumer credit card numbers as they change ... so that the merchant and consumers do not experience the inconvenience of having to update accounts each time a credit card changes. I get this ... but there is a major downside that I am surprised no one has challenged yet. 

The below may be generalized statements ... but they help paint a picture : The issuers generally do not allow the consumer to opt-in to this program The issuers do not allow the consumer to know who has been provided access to the new credit card number ( no notification ) The issuers make it difficult for consumers to manage who can hit their credit card with recurring transactions. ( you have to call and tell the issuers who to block - assuming you know which merchant has the new information ) There is a huge potential for fraud with this process, and it seems in this day and age, the idea that someone can provide my personal updated credit card information to vendors I want to stop doing business with seems outrageous. At a minimum, i should be allowed to opt in or out.

Consumer Claim: I want to make CFPB aware of another issue with Navient that has troubled me - they make it extremely difficult to find out how your payment is allocated - interest and principle - declining balance. Every other lender I know shows your prior statement balance, your payments made, how much went to interest and to principle, and then ending balance. In this way you can see if, for example, your minimum payment of {$750.00} on your student loan payment, only pays down your principle by {$79.00} dollars with the rest going to interest. My loans are consolidated with them, and I have previously raised this issue with customer service, and they had no answer other than the website was being redesigned ( this was over a year ago ). I have figured out how to do this manually, but the average student loan payer will not go to the trouble. You have to go to their " archived '' old site, then to your emails ( all my communication is electronic ), then gather your monthly statements, then write down your prior balance, payments, interest and principle, and manually review the declining balance. This is how I figured out that on many months only a tiny % of my large payment was going to principle. This to me is a very deceitful way to do business - by making it so difficult to understand the declining balance, and thus how much you are actually paying off without manually tracking it, they are encouraging everyone to remain in huge debt as long as possible. They should be made to put this on the main page of the borrowers ' loan balances - they already have the payment amounts, it would take very little programming to add this. It seems as if Navient is purposely omitting this information to keep borrowers on the hook for as long as possible since if people knew they only had a small amount going to principle they would be motivated to pay their loans off sooner.

On, or about XX/XX/2018 I responded for a solicitation from Prosper.com for a  preapproval for a {$35000.00} loan. I was approved " pending verification '' of credit, income, etc, for a {$35000.00} 60 month loan at roughly 13 %, with around a 16 % apr.

They did " verify '' {$150000.00} of income, but told me I no longer qualified for the {$35000.00}, that they could only extend {$25000.00} based on the income they could verify. They reiterated that this the new loan application for the lower amount would still be a consolidation loan, paying off {$25000.00} of my existing debt, which is the case. 

They came back with an offer which included a rate literally 2x higher then my original offer, sighting higher " debt to income ratio. '' I asked them to lower the loan amount to get the " dti '' in line with the original offer, so I could get the original rate offered ( I did the calculation, and the " dti '' ratio on the new offer of {$25000.00} with the lower income of {$150000.00}, is actually LOWER then it was with the {$35000.00} loan offer with {$200000.00} in income!! ). As I understand it, they can not charge a different rate to clients with the same credit profile ( credit score, dti ratio ). I was under the distinct impression that under federal regulations, including the FCRA and FDCPA, issuers are not to report a disputed account as delinquent during the dispute process, can not charge late fees or interest on the disputed amount ( but can retroactively apply interest should a determination be made that is not in the cardholder 's favor ), and once the investigation is completed, a cardholder must be informed of that decision and given a reasonable opportunity to make payment if the ensuing determination is not in his/her favor.

On XXXX XXXX XXXX, and XXXX XXXX XXXX, Navient 's autopay system attempted to withdraw the XXXX payment from my XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX account that I had enrolled with their autopay system. Because I did not have sufficient funds in that account, my bank charged me two {$29.00} overdraft fees, for a total of {$58.00}. 

On XXXX XXXX XXXX I contact Navient and explained the situation. I spoke with a customer service representative and eventually a supervisor. Rather than refund me for the overdraft charges that resulted from their error, the supervisor informed that although I had asked that my due date be advanced when I made my additional payment, that had no impact on their autopay system. 

The supervisor further informed me that this was my fault and Navient could not help me because I agreed to the terms listed on the website when I clicked to submit the extra payment, and that these terms state that their autopay system would continue to function despite the extra payment. After our phone call I logged on to Navient 's website to review this " disclosure. '' How it actually works is that there is a scroll box on the screen on the final step before you submit payment containing terms and a button separate from this scroll box that you click to submit the payment. Next to that button is the phrase " By selecting the Submit button below, I am acknowledging that I have read, understand, and agree to the Electronic Payment Authorization. '' Only visible after scrolling approximately 4/5ths of the way through that embedded scroll box is the phrase : *If your loan ( s ) is currently enrolled in Auto Pay, your monthly payment will continue to be deducted from your bank account as originally agreed upon. 

I honestly do not recall whether I manged to locate and read this statement prior to submitting the payment. Frankly, it should n't matter. A term referencing another agreement ( one that is not contained in the same disclosure ) should not be allowed to trump the plain meaning of primary purpose of the payment being made. 

Remember, I had just informed Navient to advance my due date and I had no desire to pay off my loan more quickly than required. If they were planning to take more money than was owed that month anyway through their autopay, they need to take additional measures beyond burying an opaque disclosure outside of the initially visible portion of a scrollbox above the submit button. 

This practice is misleading and deceptive and causes customers to inadvertently double pay or pay early on monthly payments to Navient 's advantage. Or if they do n't have the funds in their account for the unanticipated double payment, like me, they will be hit with overdraft charges.
